1: Psalm 74 is a national lament over the destruction of the temple — most likely composed in response to the Babylonian destruction of 586 BCE (cf. vv. 3-8: sanctuary defiled and burned, carved paneling smashed, meeting places burned throughout the land). It is attributed to Asaph (or the Asaphite guild) and forms part of the Asaph collection (Ps 73-83). 'O God, why have you rejected us forever? Why does your anger smolder against the sheep of your pasture?' — the central question: divine rejection that appears permanent (lanetsach, forever). The 'sheep of your pasture' is the covenant image of YHWH as shepherd and Israel as his flock (Ps 79:13; 95:7; 100:3) — the flock he now seems to have abandoned to destruction.
2: 'Remember your congregation, which you acquired long ago, the tribe of your inheritance, which you redeemed — Mount Zion, where you dwelt' — 'remember' (zekor) — the covenant appeal to divine memory as the activator of divine action. YHWH's memory of the covenant is the theological engine of Israel's restoration in Exodus (Exod 2:24: 'God remembered his covenant with Abraham'), exile, and return (Lev 26:42, 45). 'Acquired long ago' (qanita qedem) — the Exodus generation as YHWH's purchased people (Exod 15:16). 'Mount Zion, where you dwelt' — YHWH's chosen residence in Zion as the ultimate ground for the petition: restore the place you chose.
3: 'Make your way to the perpetual ruins; the enemy has wrought complete destruction in the sanctuary' — 'perpetual ruins' (mashu'ot netsach, ruins of eternity) — the temple site left as permanent wreckage. YHWH is implored to visit these ruins, to see their condition, to be moved to action. The enemy's 'complete destruction' is laid before YHWH as the evidence demanding his attention. The eyewitness detail of vv. 4-8 provides the specifics of this destruction.
4: 'Your enemies have roared in the midst of your assembly; they have set up their own banners as signs' — the enemy 'roaring' in the place of YHWH's assembly (bemo'adecha, in your meeting-place) is the profanation: the lion's roar in the sanctuary that should have been the place of YHWH's voice. 'Their own banners as signs' — the Babylonian military standards erected in the destroyed sanctuary, replacing the signs of YHWH's presence with the symbols of Babylonian conquest. The sacred space claimed by foreign military insignia is the ultimate desecration.
5: 'They were like men wielding axes to fell a dense forest' — the woodcutting imagery is both literal (they cut the cedar paneling and wood structures of the temple) and metaphorical (the temple as a great forest/garden being clear-cut). The comparison uses the image of the total, systematic destruction of a woodland — nothing is left standing. The temple's cedar panels (1 Kgs 6:9-18: cedar from Lebanon lining the inner sanctuary) made the comparison especially apt.
6: 'All its carved paneling they smashed apart with axes and hammers' — the specific violence against the temple's decoration. The 'carved paneling' (pittuchim) of the temple — the carved cherubim, palm trees, and open flowers that 1 Kgs 6:29-35 describes adorning the inner sanctuary — were systematically destroyed with axes and hammers. The detail of ax-and-hammer destruction gives the eyewitness specificity that marks this as composed by someone who witnessed or had detailed reports of the 586 BCE destruction.
7: 'They set your sanctuary on fire; they defiled the dwelling place of your name by pulling it down to the ground' — the burning of the sanctuary (mishekan shemecha, the dwelling of your name) is the act that extinguished YHWH's official presence in Israel: the divine name's dwelling destroyed. The temple was not merely a building but the place where YHWH's shem (name = his personal presence and authority) was resident (Deut 12:11; 1 Kgs 8:29: 'I have set my name there'). Its destruction left YHWH technically without an earthly residence — a theological crisis without precedent since the original establishment of the tabernacle.
8: 'They said in their hearts, We will crush them all together; they burned down every meeting place of God throughout the land' — the systematic destruction beyond the central sanctuary: the burning of 'all the meeting places of God in the land.' If this refers to pre-exilic synagogue-like gathering places (mo'adei-el), it is the earliest possible reference to local worship assemblies in Israel, suggesting that corporate worship had developed at local sites before the exile, not only in Jerusalem. The total destruction of all worship sites was the comprehensive religious suppression of the Babylonian campaign.
9: 'We see none of our signs; there is no longer a prophet; and none of us knows how long this will last' — the three absences that define the exile's theological crisis: (1) no signs (ototeinu) — no visible tokens of YHWH's presence and action; (2) no prophet — no one speaking YHWH's authoritative word (cf. 1 Sam 3:1: 'The word of the LORD was rare in those days; there was no frequent vision'); (3) no knowledge of duration ('none of us knows how long'). The exile silenced the prophetic voice and removed all signs of divine presence simultaneously — a total communication blackout from YHWH.
10: 'How long, O God, will the enemy mock you? Is the adversary to revile your name forever?' — the ad-matai ('how long?') question that characterizes the lament genre (Ps 13:1-2; 79:5; 80:4; 89:46; Hab 1:2). The lament moves from 'why?' (vv. 1, 11) to 'how long?' — from the question of causation to the question of duration. The enemies' mocking of YHWH and reviling of his name is both an offense against Israel and an offense against YHWH himself — the basis for the petition that YHWH act for his own name's sake.
11: 'Why do you hold back your hand, your right hand? Draw it out from under your garment and act!' — the provocative petition: YHWH's right hand — the divine warrior's instrument of victory — is hidden 'in your bosom' (from within your garment-fold). The image of the concealed weapon: the divine warrior has a powerful hand but has not drawn it. The boldness of the petition (essentially telling YHWH to stop hiding his hand) is characteristic of the lament genre's honesty before the covenant God.
12: 'Yet God my King is from of old, bringing salvation in the midst of the earth' — the pivot from petition to hymnic declaration. 'From of old' (mikedem) — YHWH's ancient track record of salvation: the Exodus, the Conquest, the period of the judges and kings. 'Bringing salvation in the midst of the earth' — YHWH as the active agent of historical rescue. The hymn section (vv. 12-17) recites YHWH's creative and historical acts as the grounds for the petition of vv. 18-23: if he has done these things before, he can act now.
13: 'You split the sea apart by your power; you shattered the heads of the sea monsters on the waters' — the creation/Exodus imagery of YHWH's victory over the primordial waters. 'The sea monsters' (tanninim, sea dragons) represent both the literal sea creatures of chaos and the mythological chaos figures of ANE cosmogony (Babylonian Tiamat, Ugaritic Yam). YHWH's splitting of the sea evokes both the Red Sea crossing (Exod 14:21) and the primordial creation division of the waters (Gen 1:6-7). The Psalm uses creation and Exodus as one theological event: YHWH's mastery over chaos waters.
14: 'You crushed the many heads of Leviathan; you gave his carcass as food for the creatures of the wilderness' — Leviathan was the many-headed sea dragon of Canaanite mythology (the Ugaritic Baal cycle describes Baal killing the seven-headed serpent Lotan/Leviathan). Ps 74:14 appropriates this mythological figure for YHWH's sovereign power: YHWH defeated even the chaos monster that Baal struggled against. 'Giving his carcass as food for wilderness creatures' — the complete desacralization of the chaos monster: far from being the great enemy, Leviathan becomes food for desert animals. The chaos powers are reduced to garbage.
15: 'You split open springs and streams; you dried up the ever-flowing rivers' — the two sides of YHWH's power over water: creating water sources (splitting open springs = the provision in the wilderness, Exod 17:6; Num 20:11) and destroying water sources (drying up rivers = the Jordan crossing, Josh 3:14-17, and the Red Sea crossing). YHWH commands water in both directions: he can produce it from rock and stop it in its course. The same God who controlled the primordial sea (v. 13) controls the terrestrial water systems.
16: 'The day belongs to you; the night also is yours; you established the light and the sun' — the creation hymn continues: YHWH's ownership of time (day and night) as well as space (earth and waters). In the ANE, the sun (Shamash in Babylonia) was a major deity; here the sun is YHWH's creation and instrument. The psalm asserts YHWH's sovereignty over the natural order that other nations attributed to subordinate deities.
17: 'You have fixed all the boundaries of the earth; you have made both summer and winter' — the seasonal order as YHWH's governance: summer and winter are both YHWH's creation, following the covenant promise of Gen 8:22 ('as long as the earth endures, seedtime and harvest, cold and heat, summer and winter, day and night will never cease'). The Psalm has moved from the chaos dragon (vv. 13-14) through the water systems (v. 15) through the luminaries (v. 16) to the seasonal order (v. 17): a comprehensive survey of creation's order under YHWH's sovereignty.
18: 'Remember this, O LORD: the enemy has mocked, and a foolish people has reviled your name' — the petition section resumes with 'remember' (zekor-zot) — the second covenant-memory appeal (cf. v. 2: 'remember your congregation'). The 'foolish people' (am naval) who reviles YHWH's name echoes Deut 32:21 where naval describes those without understanding. YHWH's own covenant song uses naval of Israel in rebellion; here it is applied to the enemy nations who mock YHWH.
19: 'Do not hand over your dove to the pack of enemies; do not forget the community of your poor forever' — 'your dove' (toratech, your turtledove) is an unusual metaphor for Israel: the vulnerable, gentle creature in the grip of predators. The dove as the pure animal of covenant sacrifice (Gen 8:8-12; Lev 1:14; 5:7) evokes Israel's sacrificial role and helplessness. 'The community of your poor' (adat aniyecha) — the covenant community reduced to poverty by the exile and enemy occupation, whom YHWH must not forget.
20: 'Look to your covenant, for the dark corners of the land are filled with dens of violence' — the explicit covenant appeal: look at what was promised, look at what is happening, and act on the contradiction. 'Dark corners (machashakkei-arets, dark places of the land) filled with dens of violence' — the land of Israel's habitations are now places of violent occupation. The covenant land promised as a place of rest and abundance (Deut 12:9-10) is now filled with violence. The appeal to the covenant is the appeal to YHWH's own commitments made under oath.
21: 'Do not let the oppressed be turned away in shame; let the poor and needy praise your name' — the reversal prayer: instead of shame falling on the powerless, let them praise YHWH. The 'oppressed' (dal) and 'poor and needy' (evyon ve'aniy) praise YHWH when they are vindicated — their praise is contingent on YHWH's action. The silence of the poor (shamed) and the mocking of the enemies (vv. 10, 18) would leave YHWH without any worshipers in the land. The petition for the poor's vindication is simultaneously a petition for YHWH's own worship to be restored.
22: 'Rise up, O God, and defend your cause; remember how fools mock you every single day' — 'rise up' (qumah Elohim) — the military call to YHWH to stand up and fight, as in the ancient battle cry of Num 10:35: 'Rise up, O LORD, and may your enemies be scattered.' 'Defend your cause' (rivah rivecha, plead your own case) — YHWH as the plaintiff in his own cause: the enemies' mockery of YHWH is a legal offense against YHWH himself, and YHWH is the one with standing to press charges. The psalm calls YHWH to advocate for his own honor.
23: 'Do not forget the clamor of your enemies; the uproar of those who defy you rises without ceasing' — the closing petition: YHWH must not forget the enemies' persistent noise. 'The uproar of those who defy you rises without ceasing' (sha'on qamecha oleh tamid) — the enemies' defiance is continuous and escalating. Unlike Israel's prayer, which must keep pressing YHWH to remember, the enemies' defiance never stops. The final verse holds both elements: the persistent clamor of the enemy and the implicit plea that YHWH not allow this clamor to go unanswered indefinitely.
